cert_regimes: MIL-STD-1688/248 welding quals; SUBSAFE on hull-boundary structures (subs)
rationale: Naval welding certification is general and transferable; conventional steel fabrication shops can qualify. Sub hull-boundary structures carry SUBSAFE; DDG structural is conventional. Modules/subassemblies ship, so geography is open.
sources: NAVSEA welding standards; GAO-25-106286 shipyard investment report

cert_regimes: Precision tolerances; Level I/SUBSAFE material control on propulsion-adjacent parts
rationale: CNC capability transfers from adjacent industries; BFA is actively recruiting machine shops into the maritime base. Propulsion-adjacent and pressure-boundary parts add Level I material control and longer qualification.
sources: BFA CNC machinist recruiting (advancedmanufacturing.org); Navy AM push on fittings/fasteners

cert_regimes: Metallurgical qualification; NAVSEA source approval; specialty alloys
rationale: Qualified foundry/forge base is tiny and demand is rising ~10x (Navy: castings are 'the kink in the garden hose'; 3 metals drive 50% of casting delays). Highest barrier - but also the heaviest seeding, so high-barrier-with-active-seeding reads as a long qualification path, not a closed door.
sources: Defense News 2023-09-08 (Navy supplier stabilization); GAO-24-107732 Columbia

cert_regimes: SUBSAFE pressure boundary + Level I material control (subs); valve source approval
rationale: Sub pressure-boundary piping/valves carry SUBSAFE and Level I traceability - multi-year qualification. DDG-side fluid systems are closer to conventional marine practice (effective ~3 for DDG). Valves are a named critical chokepoint with second-sourcing intent.
sources: Navy supplier development (single-vendor second-sourcing, Defense News 2023-09-08)

cert_regimes: MIL-S-901D shock, MIL-STD-167 vibration, Navy-approved insulation systems
rationale: Component quals are demanding but standardized; vendors qualify product lines once and sell across hulls. Sub-side incumbency is concentrated (turbine generators), so entry is at the component/cable/connector tier rather than the power plant tier.

cert_regimes: Shock/noise quals on fans and AC plants; conventional sheet-metal fab
rationale: Ventilation fab and fan/AC supply are conventional manufacturing with bolt-on Navy quals; Hansome's Virginia-class vane-axial fan win shows the entry path.
sources: Hansome Energy $31M NTE vane axial fan award (Defense Daily)

cert_regimes: Process certs (NAVSEA Std Items, MIL-PRF coatings); labor-driven
rationale: Marine coating/insulation is process-certified labor work with low capital barriers; special hull treatment (subs SHT) is the high-barrier exception and stays largely prime-internal.
sources: NAVSEA standard items; corpus entry pattern

rationale: Residual lane - no barrier scoring; registry cleanup queue.
